calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Apr-24-2014 Vacated

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Oct-04-2013 Set For Trial On May-12-14 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206
Plaintiff Paoli & Geerhart Llp'S Motion For Summary Judgment

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Dec-17-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF PAOLI & GEERHART LLP'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS Granted. All requests for judicial notice are granted. Plaintiff met its burden of producing admissible evidence on each element of the cause of action entitling it to judgment. (CCP 437c(p)(1).) The elements of breach of contract are: (1) the existence of a contract; (2) plaintiff's performance or excuse for nonperformance; (3) defendant's breach; and (4) damage to plaintiff. (Reichert v. General Ins. Co. (1968) 68 Cal.2d 822, 830.) As to the first element, a contract existed between the parties. (UF #2; Complaint, Ex. A.) As to the second element, plaintiff performed. (UFs #6-8; 14.) As to the third element, defendant breached by not paying 50% of the fees as required by the contract. (UF #19; Complaint, Ex. A.) Plaintiff produced admissible evidence establishing that the case went to mediation as contemplated by the unambiguous contract. (Declaration of Charles Geerhart, Ex. 3, Defendant's response to Form Interrogatory 50.6; Second Declaration of Charles Geerhart, 3-4; Declaration of Tom Paoli, 2-3; Declaration of Elise Vasquez, 2-3.) The Court finds good cause to consider the new evidence submitted with the reply papers because it directly responds to assertions made in Defendant's opposition. (Alliant Ins. Services v. Gaddy (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 1292, 1307-08.) Further, the new evidence was submitted to Defendant prior to the hearing. (Second Decl. of Geerhart, 8.) It is undisputed that Defendant "was the primary author of, and signed, the 15 page letter brief submitted to mediator Williamson dated 7/13/12." (UF # 10.) As to the fourth element, Plaintiff produced evidence showing it was damaged in the amount of $47,587.55 (excluding interest), as it was only paid approximately 23.5% of the earned fees, rather than the 50% set forth in the contract. (UF # 19; Complaint, Ex. A.) Because Plaintiff met its burden of establishing the elements, the burden shifted to Defendant to how that a triable issue of one or more material facts exists as to that cause of action or there is a defense thereto. (Id.) Defendant did not timely submit admissible evidence. Pursuant to CCP 2015.5, an out-of-state declarant must certify that a declaration is made under penalty of perjury under the laws of the State of California that the statements are true and correct. The California Supreme Court has upheld strict compliance with this statute. (Kulshrestha v. First Union Comm'l Corp., (2004) 33 Cal.4th 601, 610-611.) The Sworn Declaration of Defendant does not state it is executed under penalty of perjury or under the laws of the State of California. On December 12, 2013, Defendant filed a "Supplemental and Revised Sworn Declaration" that complies with CCP 2015.5. Even if the Court were to consider this late-filed document, Defendant has not met its burden. Defendant makes conclusory statements about the fact that a mediation did not occur, but does not set forth facts with particularity establishing this. (See e.g. Opposition, p. 5:6-7; Trailmobile, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (1989) 210 Cal.App.3d 1451, 56, holding conclusory, vague declarations are "essentially worthless.") Order signed in open court. Judge: A. James Robertson II, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Not reported.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
